title: Coil-built hanging bird feeder
subtitle: A small open-bowl feeder coiled from clay with three suspension holes for cord
excerpt: A shallow open-bowl feeder built from stacked clay coils, with three holes pierced near the rim for hanging cord. Sized to hold a handful of seed or fat balls. No kiln required; air-dry clay is sealed with two coats of outdoor varnish, which extends outdoor life to roughly one season before the body softens. A decorative seasonal piece, not a permanent garden fixture.
sourceNotes: Coil construction is one of the oldest documented ceramic-forming methods. V&A open-access collection for hanging-bowl form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or on the board, used as the building unit in coil-pot construction.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together. The standard join for any two pieces of clay that must hold.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A shallow round feeder coiled from clay, about 140 mm across and 50 mm deep, with three holes pierced near the rim for the hanging cord. Sized to hold a small scatter of seed or a single fat ball. No kiln required. The feeder uses air-dry clay sealed with two coats of outdoor varnish; outdoor life is roughly one season before damp breaks down the body, so this is a seasonal decorative piece rather than a permanent fixture.

attrs.title: Air-dry clay outdoors
attrs.body: Air-dry clay is not weatherproof even when sealed. Expect a single season of outdoor use; bring the feeder in during heavy rain or freezing weather, and re-coat or retire it once the surface softens.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay (air-dry) for stronger coil joins

items.name: Drinking straw or piping nozzle (4 mm)
items.substitution: Wooden skewer twisted to widen the hole

items.name: Waxed cotton or jute cord (3 m)

content.text: Preparation

Wedge the clay for three minutes on the board. Air pockets in a coil-built piece cause cracks in the wall as the clay dries; thorough wedging is the first line of defence. Set aside 50 g for slurry and small reinforcements. The remaining 400 g builds the base and walls.
Building the feeder
1. Cut the base

Flatten a 150 g ball into a disc 8 mm thick. Cut a circle 130 mm across. The base of a hanging feeder takes the weight of the contents and the cord pulls, so it is built thicker than a decorative bowl. Set on the board.
2. Roll and lay the first coil
Roll a piece of clay between the palms and then on the board into an even
coil

about 12 mm thick and 45 cm long. Score the top edge of the base disc with the needle tool and brush with slurry. Press the coil onto the scored edge in a circle. Cut where the ends meet and press the cut ends together. Smooth the inside of the seam down into the base with a finger or the rib.
3. Build the wall
Lay three more coils the same way,
score-and-slip
between each. Aim for a finished wall height of 50 mm and a finished wall thickness of 8 mm. The thicker wall is more forgiving of a small impact and resists cracking around the cord holes.
4. Smooth and refine

Work the inside surface of the wall with the rib in slow circular strokes to merge the coil seams into an even wall. Do the same outside, or leave the coils visible if a more obviously hand-built look is wanted. Smooth the rim with a damp sponge to soften the edge.
5. Pierce the cord holes

Mark three points around the rim, evenly spaced at 120 degrees apart. Each hole sits 8 mm below the rim, centred in the wall. Press the drinking straw or piping nozzle straight through the wall at each mark and twist gently as it comes back out. Holes pierced after the clay stiffens chip the rim; pierce while the clay is still soft.
Drying

Set the feeder on the board. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours so the rim, walls, and base dry at similar rates. Uneven drying around the pierced holes is the most common cause of rim cracking on a feeder. Remove the plastic once the clay reaches
leather-hard
. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes three to four days at this wall thickness.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the rim, base, and around the cord holes with 220-grit sandpaper. Brush off the dust. Apply two coats of exterior acrylic varnish inside and out, including the inside of each cord hole. Let the first coat dry fully (six hours minimum) before the second. A third coat on the inside surface, where seed and damp will sit, extends outdoor life.
Hanging

Cut three lengths of cord, each 80 cm. Thread one through each hole from outside to inside. Knot the inside end so it cannot pull back through; a double overhand knot is the simplest. Bring the three outside ends together above the feeder and tie them in a single knot 30 cm above the rim. The feeder hangs level when the cords are equal in length.
Care

Air-dry clay is porous; even sealed, it absorbs damp over time. Hang in a sheltered spot and bring the feeder in during heavy rain or freezing weather. Empty and wipe out any wet seed daily to keep the clay dry on the inside. Expect a single season of outdoor use before the body softens; re-coat or retire it before then. Clean with a damp cloth, never under a running tap.
Variations
Build a deeper feeder (80 mm wall) for a small water bowl rather than a seed scatter. Same coil method, more coils.
Use paper clay for stronger cord-hole edges; the cellulose fibres reinforce the pierced openings.
Press a leaf or fern into the outside wall before it stiffens. The texture catches the seal coat well.

items.cause: Holes pierced after the clay stiffened, or drying ran too fast around the openings
items.fix: Always pierce while clay is still soft; cover the feeder loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours; press fresh slurry into any small cracks before they widen

items.symptom: Coil joins separate as the feeder dries
items.cause: Joins not scored and slipped, or slurry too thin
items.fix: Score deeply with the needle tool and brush a thick clay-and-water paste across both faces before pressing the next coil on

items.cause: Cord holes were not evenly spaced, or the three cord lengths above the knot are uneven
items.fix: Measure 120 degrees apart with a paper template before piercing; cut all three cords the same length and tie the upper knot with the feeder hanging to check level

items.symptom: Sealer flakes off after one rainfall
items.cause: Standard PVA used rather than exterior varnish, or only one coat applied
items.fix: Use exterior-rated acrylic varnish; apply two coats outside and three coats inside; bring the feeder in during the heaviest rain
